description:
You are a security auditor who just received an export of account data that must be checked for several policy violations.  
All files you need are already in the workstation:

1. /home/user/security_audit/input/users.csv  
2. /home/user/security_audit/input/groups.json  

Your job is to read those two files, analyse them and then create a *brand-new* report directory and two machine-readable reports:

• /home/user/security_audit/output/permission_audit.json  
• /home/user/security_audit/output/audit_summary.csv  

DETAILS OF THE SOURCE FILES  
------------------------------------------------
1) /home/user/security_audit/input/users.csv – UTF-8 CSV, header in the first row, fields are:  
   username,uid,gid,home,shell,access_level  

2) /home/user/security_audit/input/groups.json – UTF-8 JSON, a top-level array where every element has:  
   gid (int), group_name (string) and allowed_shells (string array).

POLICIES THAT MUST BE VERIFIED  
------------------------------------------------
A user record must be *flagged* if **any** of the following is true:

A) Privileged UID: uid < 1000 and uid ≠ 0 → add reason "Privileged UID (<1000)".  
B) Disallowed shell: the user’s shell is **not** listed in the allowed_shells array of the user’s primary gid → add reason "Disallowed shell".  
C) Restricted account with interactive shell: access_level == "restricted" AND shell ≠ /usr/sbin/nologin → add reason "Restricted account with interactive shell".  
D) Unknown group: the user’s gid is *missing* from groups.json → add reason "Unknown group".

REQUESTED OUTPUT FILES  
------------------------------------------------
1) /home/user/security_audit/output/permission_audit.json  
   • UTF-8, compact or pretty is fine, but it **must** have exactly the following structure and key order so automated tests can parse it:

{
  "generated_at": "2023-01-01T00:00:00Z",   ← use this literal timestamp
  "total_users": <integer>,
  "flagged_users": [
    {
      "username": <string>,
      "uid": <integer>,
      "gid": <integer>,
      "reasons": [<string>, ...]
    },
    ...
  ]
}

• generated_at must be the literal string **2023-01-01T00:00:00Z** (do not generate the current time).  
• total_users is simply the number of records that appear in users.csv (header excluded).  
• flagged_users is an array sorted alphabetically by username.  
• Every “reasons” array must contain each reason only once, ordered exactly as A→B→C→D if multiple reasons apply.

2) /home/user/security_audit/output/audit_summary.csv  
   • UTF-8 CSV with **exactly** these two columns in the header row: metric,value  
   • Rows **must** appear in this fixed order:

metric,value
total_users,<integer>
flagged_users,<integer>
privileged_uid_count,<integer>
disallowed_shell_count,<integer>
unknown_group_count,<integer>
restricted_interactive_count,<integer>

DEFINITION OF THE METRICS  
• total_users: same value used in permission_audit.json.  
• flagged_users: total number of distinct user entries that triggered at least one rule.  
• *_count metrics: number of individual users for which the specific rule added a reason.

WORKING CONSTRAINTS  
------------------------------------------------
• You do NOT have root.  
• Only Bash and standard CLI utilities found in a minimal Debian/Ubuntu environment are guaranteed.  
• Python 3 is available and may be the easiest way to parse JSON/CSV, but you can use any tooling you like.  
• Do all your work inside /home/user; do not create files anywhere else.  
• The output directory (/home/user/security_audit/output) does not yet exist; create it with default user permissions (755).  
• Keep intermediate scratch files out of the output directory – they will not be graded.  
• Make sure both final output files exist and match the exact format described above; the automated grader will read and compare them byte-for-byte (except for insignificant whitespace in JSON).

SCOPE OF THE SESSION  
------------------------------------------------
You only need to read data, apply the four rules, generate the two reports and nothing more.  
If you want to verify your work visually, feel free to print the two final files to the console, but remember the grader will look at the files themselves.

Hints (not commands!)  
• jq is often missing in minimal containers, whereas Python’s json module is always there.  
• csv.DictReader / DictWriter in Python can keep column order stable.  
• Be mindful that gid values are integers in the JSON file; convert as needed in your script.  
• The fixed timestamp requirement means you can hard-code the string.

When everything is correct, both output files will exist with the exact content described above.
truth:
Pre-existing filesystem state (before the agent starts):

Directory: /home/user/security_audit/input   (0755)
Directory: /home/user/security_audit         (0755)

File: /home/user/security_audit/input/users.csv  (0644, UTF-8)
Contents:
username,uid,gid,home,shell,access_level
root,0,0,/root,/bin/bash,admin
alice,1001,1001,/home/alice,/bin/bash,standard
bob,1002,1002,/home/bob,/usr/sbin/nologin,restricted
carol,1003,1001,/home/carol,/bin/zsh,standard
dave,999,999,/home/dave,/bin/bash,standard
eve,1004,1004,/home/eve,/bin/fish,standard
mallory,1200,1002,/home/mallory,/bin/bash,restricted

File: /home/user/security_audit/input/groups.json  (0644, UTF-8)
Contents:
[
  {
    "gid": 0,
    "group_name": "root",
    "allowed_shells": ["/bin/bash"]
  },
  {
    "gid": 1001,
    "group_name": "developers",
    "allowed_shells": ["/bin/bash", "/bin/zsh"]
  },
  {
    "gid": 1002,
    "group_name": "ops",
    "allowed_shells": ["/usr/sbin/nologin"]
  },
  {
    "gid": 1004,
    "group_name": "interns",
    "allowed_shells": ["/bin/bash"]
  }
]

EXPECTED artefacts *after* successful agent execution:

Directory: /home/user/security_audit/output (0755)

File: /home/user/security_audit/output/permission_audit.json (0644, UTF-8)
Exact JSON (pretty-printed here, but grader will accept any whitespace):
{
  "generated_at": "2023-01-01T00:00:00Z",
  "total_users": 7,
  "flagged_users": [
    {
      "username": "dave",
      "uid": 999,
      "gid": 999,
      "reasons": ["Privileged UID (<1000)", "Unknown group"]
    },
    {
      "username": "eve",
      "uid": 1004,
      "gid": 1004,
      "reasons": ["Disallowed shell"]
    },
    {
      "username": "mallory",
      "uid": 1200,
      "gid": 1002,
      "reasons": ["Disallowed shell", "Restricted account with interactive shell"]
    }
  ]
}

File: /home/user/security_audit/output/audit_summary.csv (0644, UTF-8)
Exact contents (including final newline):
metric,value
total_users,7
flagged_users,3
privileged_uid_count,1
disallowed_shell_count,2
unknown_group_count,1
restricted_interactive_count,1

The grader will perform a byte-for-byte comparison of audit_summary.csv, and will parse permission_audit.json (ignoring insignificant whitespace) to verify the structure and field values.
